notes: Smoothed weighted average of the middle third (35th-65th percentile) of home Zestimates in a region. Measures the typical home value -- not a strict median, not a strict mean. Updated monthly. Uses neural Zestimate methodology since 2023.
Part of Zillow's stable cut-type key (e.g. "uc_sfrcondo"). Not separately defined in the public methodology -- treat as opaque.

meaning: All homes -- single-family residences plus condos and co-ops combined

notes: Bucket includes homes with 5 or more bedrooms.
Filename label is approximate. Zillow's published methodology defines the bottom tier as the 5th-35th percentile of home values in the metro -- not literally 0-33. Tails are trimmed.
Filename label is approximate. Zillow's methodology defines the middle tier as the 35th-65th percentile of home values in the metro. Most-cited variant for general analysis.
Filename label is approximate. Zillow's methodology defines the top tier as the 65th-95th percentile of home values in the metro. Tails are trimmed.
Smoothing reduces month-to-month noise; seasonal adjustment removes systematic monthly seasonality so trends are easier to read. Almost every modeling-relevant ZHVI file is sm_sa.

meaning: Raw monthly series -- NOT smoothed and NOT seasonally adjusted
notes: Mixes poorly with sm_sa series in the same model (different noise characteristics). Removed from this project for that reason.
One value per region per calendar month.
Group of counties that economically function as one urban region. ~390 metros in the US. Coarser than counties; many counties combine into one metro row.

meaning: Zillow's stable numeric ID for the region
notes: Use this -- not RegionName -- as the join key against other Zillow files.

meaning: National rank of the region by population/size
notes: 1 = largest. Useful for filtering to top-N metros if needed.
For these files always "msa" (or "country" for the US-total row at the top).

meaning: Two-letter state abbreviation of the metro's primary state
notes: Empty for the country-level row. A metro can span multiple states (e.g. NYC); only the primary state is shown.
